verses.commentary: The promises of divine sonship motivate holistic purification. Body and spirit both require cleansing; holiness is perfected out of reverence, not fear of punishment but awe of God.
Paul repeats his appeal for the Corinthians to make room in their hearts. His defense of integrity (wronged no one, corrupted no one, exploited no one) clears the way for restored affection.
The intention is not condemnation but reaffirmation. The Corinthians are so deeply in Paul's heart that he would live or die with them. This is covenant language applied to ministry relationships.
Paul's frankness and pride in them overflow into encouragement. Even in troubles, his joy knows no bounds because of the Corinthians' response.
Macedonia brought no rest, only external conflicts and internal fears. The apostolic life includes persistent anxiety and pressure from multiple directions simultaneously.
God comforts the downcast specifically through human presence. Titus's arrival was God's method of consolation, demonstrating that divine comfort often uses embodied messengers.
The comfort was both Titus's presence and the good report he brought. The Corinthians' longing, sorrow, and concern for Paul became the source of even greater joy.
Paul does not regret the letter, though he initially did. Temporary hurt was acceptable because it achieved lasting good. Pastoral letters may wound in order to heal.
Joy comes not from causing sorrow but from sorrow leading to repentance. Godly sorrow functions properly when it produces the intended result without causing harm.
Godly sorrow versus worldly sorrow: one leads to repentance and salvation without regret; the other produces death. The direction of sorrow determines its fruit.

Godly sorrow produced earnestness, eagerness to clear themselves, indignation, alarm, longing, concern, readiness for justice. The seven outcomes demonstrate genuine repentance's comprehensive transformation.
The letter's purpose was not primarily about the wrongdoer or the wronged party but to reveal to the Corinthians their own devotion to Paul before God.
Encouragement is multiplied by seeing Titus's joy. His spirit was refreshed by the Corinthians, adding a second layer of consolation beyond the initial encouragement.
Paul's boasting about the Corinthians to Titus was vindicated. Just as everything he said to them was true, so his praise of them to Titus proved true.
Titus's affection grows greater as he remembers their obedience and how they received him with fear and trembling. Proper reception intensifies relational affection.
Complete confidence in them closes the chapter. The journey from anxiety to joy to confidence demonstrates how repentance restores relationship and trust.
